nativeRange.habitat: Clear, slow-moving to still water in the upper water column; pools and backwaters with floating or emergent bankside vegetation; occasionally lentic habitats including the Lake Maracaibo basin

decorPreferences: floating plants or overhanging emergent vegetation for surface cover
large open swimming corridor along the surface
driftwood and roots along sides and back for visual breaks
tightly fitting lid — these fish are powerful jumpers
subdued or indirect lighting tolerated well

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in the wild; in captivity accepts frozen whitebait, prawn, mussel, krill, and chopped earthworms. Juveniles often require live feeder fish or guppies initially; weaning onto high-quality frozen and vitamin-enriched carnivore pellets is achievable with patience. Avoid exclusively feeder fish to prevent nutritional deficiency and disease introduction. Mammalian or avian meat (beef heart, chicken) should not be offered. Trophic level 4.2 confirmed on FishBase.

goodWith: silver dollars (Metynnis spp.) — similar size, not swallowable
severums (Heros spp.) and chocolate cichlids (Hypselecara temporalis)
avoidWith: small fish that fit in the mouth (<5 cm) — will be eaten
fin-nipping species (tiger barbs, serpae tetras) — stress and fin damage
aggressive cichlids (green terror, red devil) — conflict
surface-dwelling fish competing for the same swim zone
delicate or slow species likely to be harassed

summary: A sleek, silver surface-dweller from Colombia and Venezuela, the Hujeta Gar is a characiform pike-characin — not a true gar (Lepisosteidae). Reaching ~25 cm, it is a cooperative schooling piscivore demanding a tightly covered 400 L+ tank, frozen-meaty diet, and excellent water quality.
careNotes:
The Hujeta Gar (Ctenolucius hujeta) belongs to the family Ctenoluciidae — the pike-characins — and is a characiform rather than a true gar (family Lepisosteidae); the common name is misleading. Its natural range spans the Río Magdalena and Río Sinú drainages of northern Colombia and the Lake Maracaibo basin of northwestern Venezuela, where it inhabits the upper water column of clear, slow-moving rivers, pools, and lakes.

Tank setup should prioritise horizontal swimming space — a minimum footprint of 180 × 60 cm (roughly 400 L) is recommended for a group of five or more. Surface cover in the form of floating plants (water lettuce, Amazon frogbit) or overhanging branches is important; this species is wary in bare open tanks. A tightly fitting lid with no gaps is essential: Hujeta Gars are powerful, explosive jumpers and will exit the tank through any opening when startled or spooked. Efficient external canister filtration and 30–50% weekly water changes are strongly advised to manage the waste output of a piscivorous group.

Water chemistry should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–15, kH 1–8) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.0–7.5); temperature 22–28°C. They adapt to a moderately wide range but react poorly to sudden swings or accumulated organics. Avoid copper-based treatments.

Diet in captivity centres on frozen meaty foods: whitebait, prawn, krill, mussel, and chopped earthworms. Juveniles often demand live feeder guppies or river shrimp initially; systematic weaning onto frozen and prepared carnivore pellets is achievable over several weeks. Feed once or twice daily; avoid overfeeding as it rapidly degrades water quality.

Keep in groups of five or more — solo or paired individuals become nervous, refuse food, and are more prone to jumping. Juveniles display clear schooling behaviour; adults form loose cooperative hunting aggregations. Conspecific aggression is low provided tankmates are too large to eat. Suitable companions include silver dollars, large peaceful cichlids, and robust catfish; any fish small enough to swallow will be predated.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria. SeriouslyFish reports spawning occurs at the water surface; pairs release over 1,000 adhesive eggs that hatch in approximately 20 hours at 27°C. Fry are active immediately and require frequent feeding (three or more small meals daily) to suppress cannibalism. Conditioning requires live foods and pristine water quality. No specialised substrate is needed but the spawning zone must be kept clear. Captive breeding records are scarce; this is considered advanced-level breeding.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Ctenolucius hujeta
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (200–250 mm SL), temperature (22–28°C), pH (5.0–7.5), hardness (18–268 ppm ≈ 1–15 dGH), native range (Lake Maracaibo basin and Río Magdalena/Sinú drainages), minimum tank dimensions (180 × 60 cm base), schooling grouping (4+), surface-dwelling habitat notes, diet (frozen/live meaty foods), breeding (surface spawning, 1000+ eggs, ~20 h hatch at 27°C), and escape-risk warning (excellent jumpers, tight-fitting cover required).

additional.name: FishBase — Ctenolucius hujeta (ID 12407)
additional.notes: Cross-checked: maximum SL 26.0 cm, weight 119.20 g, temperature 22–25°C, native range (Río Magdalena and Río Sinú, Colombia; rivers draining Lago de Maracaibo, Venezuela), taxonomy (Characiformes, Ctenoluciidae), trophic level 4.2 (confirming piscivore), IUCN Least Concern, medium resilience with minimum population doubling time 1.4–4.4 years.

additional.name: Gensou Premium Aquascaping — Freshwater Barracuda Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Confirmed temperature 25–28°C, pH 6.5–7.5, gH 4–12, minimum tank 550 L / 180 cm length, adult size 25–30 cm, lifespan 8–12 years, grouping minimum 5 (6+ optimal), high escape risk with tight-fitting lids required, cooperative hunting behaviour in groups, good tankmates (silver dollars, large Congo tetras, Corydoras sterbai, severums), poor tankmates (tiger barbs, aggressive cichlids).

additional.name: Pet Zone Tropical Fish — Keeping the Hujeta Gar
additional.notes: Trade hobbyist source. Confirmed minimum 100 gal (~380 L) for a single specimen, temperature 72–82°F (22–28°C), pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 10–25 dGH, carnivore diet (krill, shrimp, crickets, small fish), high waste production requiring robust filtration. Used to corroborate tank-size floor and parameter ranges.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Ctenolucius hujeta
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Ctenoluciidae, order Characiformes, original description by Valenciennes 1850 as Xiphostoma hujeta), common names (silver gar, rocket gar, hujeta gar, freshwater barracuda), and range (Lake Maracaibo basin, northwestern Venezuela; Río Magdalena, northern Colombia). Confirmed this is NOT a true gar (Lepisosteidae).

fish.breedingNotes: Surface spawning with 1,000+ adhesive eggs; hatch in ~20 hours at 27°C. Fry require three daily feedings to suppress cannibalism. Conditioning on live foods and pristine water quality essential. Rarely achieved in home aquaria.
